body.creator: g
body.value: Throughout this sequence, close-ups of piercing, merging, and layering stress the simultaneously destructive and constructive transformation of raw material into something new—a garment.

body.creator: g
body.value: Cassie Davies-Strodder reports on the standard fashion house’s staffing: “In 1946, The Strand magazine documented staffing of an average London couture house. This included two female vendeuses, or saleswomen who catered for the client’s every need, taking orders, organizing fittings and advising on what to purchase. The workrooms were headed by a tailor (usually a man) or a dress fitter; under them were largely female teams of secondes, who distributed work, and the ‘hands,’ who in turn passed on work to the ‘juniors’ (about 40 to 60 of them). If the house was successful they might have employed two or more ‘house’ models to show the garments. As well as the many female staff employed in the workrooms and salesrooms, women also acted as chief advisors and even business partners. These jobs were often reserved for a close family member, and several leading couturiers, including Norman Hartnell, employed their sisters in these roles” (Davies-Strodder, “The New Bloom of Couture). While the film doesn’t dwell too long on the “hands” (the workers of the House of Woodcock) and their lives, the camera repeatedly focuses on the manual labor of crafting the dresses, particularly through these extended close-ups. The body, whether that of the model or the designer, becomes a manifestation of their desires, inner states, and relationships.
